text: The surgeon begins the operation with a teardrop incision extending cranially and caudally in the midline. The perineal sinus is dissected down to its base using a combination of sharp dissection and electrocautery. The mobilized sinus is carefully examined to ensure complete clearance of affected tissue, often necessitating the excision of the base of the coccyx.

The surgeon begins constructing the eye gap flap by defining a skin paddle centered around the inferior gluteal artery perforation, located between the posterior superior iliac spine and the ischial tuberosity at the piriformis level. Incisions are made through the subcutaneous and fascial layers down to the gluteus maximus muscle, carefully avoiding damage to underlying structures. The perforating vessels are identified and mobilized from the gluteus maximus muscle by dissecting along the fiber travel line. The flap is mobilized laterally to medially in the sub-facial layer, ensuring good rotation for the flap.

The surgeon advances the eye gap flap into the perineal defect, identifying and de-epithelializing the buried portion. Multiple perforating vessels, including an artery and a vein, are visible passing through the gluteus maximus muscle into the flap. The flap is secured superiorly to the superficial part of the sacrum and inferiorly to the pelvic inlet, ensuring well-vascularized tissue at the wound base. A suction drain is placed underneath the flap, extending to the base of the perineal defect, and the wound is closed in layers using a VY fashion.
